---
title: "Exercise: parallel reduction (sum) with workgroup shared memory"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Compute the **sum of an `f32` array** on the GPU using the classic **parallel-reduction** pattern: each workgroup loads its slice into shared memory, performs a tree-shaped reduction within the workgroup, and writes one partial sum to global memory. The host then sums the (small) array of partials.

This is the *right* way to do GC content, k-mer counts, mean expression — any "sum of N numbers" — on the GPU. The atomic version from exercise 3 was fine for getting the answer; this is the version you would actually ship.

By the time `cargo test` passes you will have used `var<workgroup>` shared memory, `workgroupBarrier()`, the `@builtin(local_invocation_id)` and `@builtin(workgroup_id)` indices, and seen how a single kernel call shrinks a million floats to ~16 000 in one pass.

## Background — why a tree reduction beats a single atomic

A single atomic counter (`atomicAdd(&total, x[i])`) over `n = 10⁷` floats: every thread contends on one address. Throughput drops to roughly the atomic unit's rate (~10⁹ ops/s), so the kernel takes ~10 ms — most of which is *waiting* in the atomic queue.

A tree reduction:

- Each workgroup of 64 threads loads 64 floats into shared memory (~5 ns per load).
- 6 rounds of intra-workgroup pairwise sums (each round halves the live threads). Total per workgroup: ~30 ns.
- Each workgroup writes ONE partial sum to global memory.
- The host sees `n / 64` partial sums to add up — for `n = 10⁷` that's ~156 000 floats, a millisecond on the CPU.

Total: well under 1 ms. **~10× faster** than the atomic version, with almost zero contention.

## The shader

```wgsl
@group(0) @binding(0) var<storage, read>       input:  array<f32>;
@group(0) @binding(1) var<uniform>             info:   Info;
@group(0) @binding(2) var<storage, read_write> partials: array<f32>;

struct Info { n: u32, _pad: vec3<u32> }

var<workgroup> tile: array<f32, 64>;

@compute @workgroup_size(64)
fn main(
    @builtin(global_invocation_id) gid: vec3<u32>,
    @builtin(local_invocation_id)  lid: vec3<u32>,
    @builtin(workgroup_id)         wid: vec3<u32>,
) {
    // Load one input element per thread into shared memory; pad with 0 if past end.
    let i = gid.x;
    tile[lid.x] = select(0.0, input[i], i < info.n);
    workgroupBarrier();

    // Tree reduction: 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1.
    var stride: u32 = 32u;
    loop {
        if (stride == 0u) { break; }
        if (lid.x < stride) { tile[lid.x] = tile[lid.x] + tile[lid.x + stride]; }
        workgroupBarrier();
        stride = stride / 2u;
    }

    // Thread 0 of each workgroup writes the per-workgroup sum.
    if (lid.x == 0u) { partials[wid.x] = tile[0]; }
}
```

The pieces:

- `var<workgroup> tile: array<f32, 64>` — 256 bytes of shared memory per workgroup, fast.
- `workgroupBarrier()` — waits until every thread in the workgroup has reached the call.
- `select(0.0, input[i], i < info.n)` — branchless "x[i] if in bounds else 0.0". Cleaner than an `if`.
- Tree reduction: 64 → 32 → 16 → 8 → 4 → 2 → 1, with a barrier between each step. After all rounds, `tile[0]` holds the sum of the 64 loaded values.

## The exercise

Open `day12/ex-reduction/`. The shader is provided. Your job:

1. Allocate the **partials output buffer** of the right size — `n_workgroups` `f32`s.
2. After dispatch and read-back, **sum the partials on the host** to get the final total.
3. Make the unit tests pass — including the empty-input case (sum of 0 elements = 0.0).

Tests check the sum against a CPU reference on several random arrays of varying length.

## How to run

```bash
cd day12/ex-reduction
cargo test
cargo run --release        # sum of 1 M floats; prints CPU vs GPU result and timing
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — sizing the partials buffer"}
The partials buffer holds one `f32` per workgroup. Workgroup count is `n.div_ceil(64)`:

```rust
let workgroups = n.div_ceil(64) as u32;
let partials_size = (workgroups as u64) * std::mem::size_of::<f32>() as u64;
let partials_buf = device.create_buffer(&wgpu::BufferDescriptor {
    label: Some("partials"),
    size: partials_size.max(4),                  // at least 4 bytes; wgpu doesn't allow 0-size buffers
    usage: wgpu::BufferUsages::STORAGE | wgpu::BufferUsages::COPY_SRC,
    mapped_at_creation: false,
});
```

For `n = 0` you bypass dispatch entirely and return `0.0` — saves you from edge-cases in the shader.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — sum the partials on the host"}
After read-back you have a small `Vec<f32>` of partials. Final sum is just:

```rust
let total: f32 = partials.iter().sum();
```

For very large inputs (millions of workgroups) you can run a *second pass* of the same kernel on the partials. For the sizes in this exercise the host sum is fine.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — floating-point caveat"}
GPU reductions are not bit-identical to a sequential CPU sum, because the order of additions differs and `f32` addition is non-associative. The test allows a relative tolerance of ~1e-5. Don't be alarmed if the GPU result differs by a few ULPs from the naive Rust sum.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
See `day12/ex-reduction-solution/src/main.rs`. The partials buffer is sized from the workgroup count; final sum is a one-line `.iter().sum::<f32>()` after read-back.
:::

## Where this leads

- **Every "sum N things" GPU kernel uses this shape.** Mean expression per gene, GC content over a 100 MB buffer, total alignment score — all of them are this kernel with a different input.
- **Burn's reductions** (`tensor.sum()`, `tensor.mean()`, `tensor.max()`) compile to exactly this pattern under the hood, often with a recursive second pass when the partials don't fit in one workgroup.
- **You can compose tree reductions** with the per-element work of an earlier exercise — e.g. exercise 3 (GC content) can be rewritten as "score per byte → tree reduction over scores", removing the atomic and gaining ~10× throughput.
